flashcards.front: What are the three histological layers found in all major arteries?
flashcards.back: Tunica intima (inner), tunica media (middle), and tunica adventitia (outer) [2].

flashcards.front: Why is the integrity of the vascular endothelium considered of 'paramount importance'?
flashcards.back: Damage to the endothelium is the most critical event in the initiation of thrombus formation [2].

flashcards.front: How do the tunica intima and the inner third of the media receive nutrition?
flashcards.back: By direct diffusion from the blood present in the vessel lumen [11].

flashcards.front: What is the primary source of nutrition for the outer two-thirds of the media and the adventitia?
flashcards.back: Vasa vasora (vessels of vessels) arising from the parent artery [11].

flashcards.front: What is the defining internal diameter of an arteriole?
What histological feature distinguishes veins from arteries regarding the tunica media?
flashcards.back: The media in veins contains very few smooth muscle cells and abundant collagen; elastic laminae are not clearly organized [13, 14].

flashcards.front: Define 'Arteriosclerosis'.
flashcards.back: A general term for conditions involving the thickening and hardening of arterial walls [3].

flashcards.front: Which morphologic entity of arteriosclerosis is characterized by 'onion-skin' lesions?
flashcards.back: Hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis, typically seen in malignant hypertension [15, 16].

flashcards.front: What is Mönckeberg’s arteriosclerosis (Medial calcific sclerosis)?
flashcards.back: Calcification of the media of large/medium muscular arteries in persons over 50; it is typically an incidental finding of little clinical significance because it doesn't narrow the lumen [17-19].

flashcards.front: What is the origin of the term 'Atherosclerosis'?
flashcards.back: 'Athero' means porridge (soft lipid core) and 'sclerosis' means scarring (connective tissue in the plaque) [19].

flashcards.front: List the four major modifiable risk factors for atherosclerosis.
flashcards.back: Dyslipidaemias, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and smoking [20, 21].

flashcards.front: Which lipoprotein is known as 'good cholesterol' and why?
flashcards.back: HDL (High-density lipoprotein) because it is protective against atherosclerosis [22, 23].

flashcards.front: What is the 'Reaction-to-Injury' hypothesis in atherosclerosis?
flashcards.back: The theory that endothelial injury is the triggering event, followed by smooth muscle cell proliferation and monocyte-macrophage infiltration [24-26].

flashcards.front: What are 'Foam cells'?
flashcards.back: Lipid-laden macrophages formed when monocytes take up oxidized LDL in the tunica intima [27].

flashcards.front: What are the precursor lesions of atheromatous plaques that can appear in the first year of life?
flashcards.back: Fatty streaks and dots [28, 29].

flashcards.front: Identify the two main components of a fully developed atheromatous plaque.
flashcards.back: A firm, white fibrous cap and a central soft core composed of yellow porridge-like lipid material [30, 31].

flashcards.front: What are the 'complicated lesions' of atherosclerosis that cause the most serious harm?
flashcards.back: Calcification, ulceration, thrombosis, hemorrhage, and aneurysmal dilatation [32, 33].

flashcards.front: Critical Thinking: Why is atherosclerosis more severe at arterial bifurcations?
flashcards.back: Bifurcations are points of high shear stress and hemodynamic forces, which more easily cause endothelial injury [26].

flashcards.front: Distinguish c-ANCA from p-ANCA patterns.
flashcards.back: c-ANCA (cytoplasmic) is specific for Proteinase-3 and Wegener’s granulomatosis; p-ANCA (perinuclear) is specific for myeloperoxidase and microscopic polyarteritis [34].

flashcards.front: Which vasculitis is known as 'Pulseless Disease'?
flashcards.back: Takayasu’s arteritis, which primarily affects the aorta and its major branches in young women [35].

flashcards.front: What is the pathognomonic clinical feature of Wegener’s granulomatosis?
flashcards.back: A triad of necrotizing granulomas of the respiratory tract, focal necrotising vasculitis, and necrotising glomerulonephritis [36, 37].

flashcards.front: Define Buerger’s disease (Thromboangiitis obliterans).
flashcards.back: An occlusive inflammatory disease of small/medium arteries and veins in the extremities, strongly associated with heavy smoking [38, 39].

flashcards.front: How does Raynaud’s disease differ from Raynaud’s phenomenon?
flashcards.back: Raynaud’s disease is a primary functional disorder in healthy young females; Raynaud’s phenomenon is secondary to an underlying disease like scleroderma or atherosclerosis [40, 41].

flashcards.front: Define 'True Aneurysm' vs. 'False Aneurysm'.
flashcards.back: True: involves all layers of a normal vessel wall. False: has a fibrous wall, often following trauma [42].

flashcards.front: Where is the most common site for an atherosclerotic aneurysm?
flashcards.back: The abdominal aorta, typically infra-renal [43, 44].

flashcards.front: What is a 'Cor Bovinum'?
flashcards.back: A massively enlarged heart (like an ox heart) caused by aortic incompetence in syphilitic heart disease [45].

flashcards.front: Explain the pathogenesis of a 'Dissecting Aneurysm'.
flashcards.back: Blood enters through an intimal tear and separates (dissects) the media longitudinally [46].

flashcards.front: What is 'Cystic Medial Necrosis of Erdheim'?
flashcards.back: A degenerative condition of the media (seen in Marfan’s) that predisposes the aorta to dissection [47, 48].

flashcards.front: Distinguish Stanford Type A from Type B aortic dissections.
flashcards.back: Type A (Proximal) involves the ascending aorta; Type B (Distal) is limited to the descending aorta [49, 50].

flashcards.front: What are the 'Three Ts' of venous thrombosis initiation?
flashcards.back: Endothelial damage, alteration in blood composition, and venous stasis (Virchow’s triad) [51].

flashcards.front: What is Trousseau’s syndrome (Thrombophlebitis migrans)?
flashcards.back: Multiple migratory venous thrombi, often associated with disseminated visceral cancers as a paraneoplastic syndrome [52].

flashcards.front: Define Milroy’s disease.
flashcards.back: A familial hereditary form of congenital lymphoedema inherited as an autosomal dominant trait [53].

flashcards.front: What is the 'Strawberry Birthmark'?
flashcards.back: A capillary haemangioma common in infancy that often regresses spontaneously [54, 55].

flashcards.front: Where are Glomus tumors most commonly found?
flashcards.back: Under the nail of fingers or toes; they are characterized by extreme pain [56].

flashcards.front: What is the etiologic agent of Bacillary Angiomatosis in AIDS patients?
flashcards.back: Bartonella bacilli [57].

flashcards.front: What are the four forms of Kaposi’s sarcoma?
flashcards.back: Classic (European), African (Endemic), Epidemic (AIDS-associated), and Renal transplant-associated [10, 58, 59].

flashcards.front: Which virus is consistently associated with Kaposi’s sarcoma?
flashcards.back: Human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8) [59, 60].

flashcards.front: Case Study Analysis: A 65-year-old male smoker with intermittent leg pain and a small toe ulcer. Likely diagnosis?
flashcards.back: Peripheral vascular disease due to atherosclerosis; the pain is 'intermittent claudication' [5, 61].

flashcards.front: Bias Recognition: Why shouldn't you ignore 'benign' medial calcification on an X-ray?
flashcards.back: Mönckeberg's medial calcific sclerosis is harmless, but clinicians may falsely attribute symptoms to it, missing coexistent atherosclerosis [18, 19].

flashcards.front: Clinical reasoning: Why is a 'Double-barrel aorta' considered a signs of survival in dissection?
flashcards.back: It indicates a second distal intimal tear occurred, allowing blood to re-enter the true lumen, potentially stabilizing the patient [49].

flashcards.front: University Question: Contrast Syphilitic Aortitis and Aortic Atheroma sites.
flashcards.back: Syphilitic: Ascending aorta and arch; absent below diaphragm. Atheroma: Abdominal aorta; increases from arch to bifurcation [62].

flashcards.front: USMLE Pattern: A patient presents with fever, sinusitis, and hematuria. Serum is c-ANCA positive. Likely diagnosis?
flashcards.back: Wegener’s granulomatosis [34, 37].

flashcards.front: PLAB Pattern: A young child presents with a high fever, oral erosions, and a skin rash. Imaging shows coronary aneurysms. Likely diagnosis?
flashcards.back: Kawasaki’s disease [63].

flashcards.front: Decision-Making: A patient with a known abdominal aortic aneurysm presents with sudden, excruciating back pain and hypotension. Immediate step?
flashcards.back: Emergency surgical consultation for suspected aneurysm rupture [7, 64].

flashcards.front: Core Mechanism: How does smoking cause hypoxia in the arterial wall?
flashcards.back: Accumulation of carbon monoxide produces carboxyhaemoglobin, favoring atherosclerosis [65].

flashcards.front: Diagnostic Morphology: What is the 'Tree-bark' appearance in the aorta characteristic of?
flashcards.back: Syphilitic aortitis (due to wrinkled normal intima between pearly-white thickenings) [66, 67].

flashcards.front: Problem Solving: How do you differentiate Buerger's disease from Atherosclerosis histologically?
flashcards.back: Buerger's has microabscesses in thrombi and an intact internal elastic lamina; atherosclerosis lacks these and features lipid aggregates [68].

flashcards.front: Reflective Question: Why might atherosclerosis be called a 'multifactorial process'?
flashcards.back: Because it involves lipids, hemodynamic stress, inflammatory cells (monocytes), and smooth muscle proliferation [29, 69].

flashcards.front: University Essay Point: Explain the role of PDGF in atherogenesis.
flashcards.back: Platelet-derived growth factor stimulates the proliferation and migration of smooth muscle cells from the media into the intima [70].

flashcards.front: USMLE High-Yield: Which vascular tumor is associated with carcinogens like polyvinyl chloride and Thorotrast?
flashcards.back: Hepatic angiosarcoma [71].

flashcards.front: Core Definition: What is 'Trousseau's Syndrome'?
flashcards.back: Migratory thrombophlebitis—venous thrombi that disappear from one site and appear at another, indicating a disseminated visceral cancer [52].

flashcards.front: Clinical Feature: What defines 'Superior Vena Caval Syndrome'?
flashcards.back: Obstruction of the SVC causing face/neck edema, dilated neck veins, and visual disturbances [72].

flashcards.front: Mechanism: Why does Marfan's syndrome lead to aortic dissection?
flashcards.back: A genetic defect in fibrillin causes medial weakness (cystic medial necrosis), making the aorta susceptible to intimal tears [47].

flashcards.front: Clinical Sign: Define 'Intermittent Claudication'.
flashcards.back: Intense limb pain during walking caused by ischemia from atherosclerotic narrowing; relieved by rest [39, 61].

flashcards.front: Morphology: What are 'Birbeck granules' (Reference link to Ch 12)?
flashcards.back: Rod/tennis-racket-shaped inclusions found in Langerhans cell histiocytosis (not explicitly detailed here, but standard medical knowledge for differential diagnosis of vascular lesions) [Source context: Tumors section 127].
